text:
Peter Lynch favored the PEG ratio because it ties valuation to growth.

I prefer using it over a 2026–2028 window so valuation adjusts for how fast the business is actually compounding.

For example, $GOOGL may trade at ~26x forward earnings but EPS is expected to grow from $11.51 in 2026 to $15.31 in 2028 implying ~15% earnings CAGR so their PEG is ~1.7x.

PETER LYNCH’S FAVORITE METRIC IS THE PEG RATIO

PEG < 1 usually means mispriced growth
PEG > 2 starts to push into the danger zone

Popular names right now:
• $TSLA ~3.5x

Anduril landing a $20B government contract shows how defense budgets are starting to flow into the new-age defense names like $PLTR, $ONDS, $KRKNF, $RKLB & $IONQ. 

Legacy defense moved slow & billed more but this new generation gets paid to move fast, ship real tech & iterate.

$IREN is expected to generate over $1B in operating profits by 2028 from the 50K $NVDA B300 GPUs on order bringing its total fleet to 150K.

The company controls ~4.5 GW of secured power capacity while needing only ~500 MW to support its $3.7B ARR target by year end.

That is roughly a 10:1 power advantage few neocloud players can replicate quickly.

$MELI CEO says fintech is the company’s biggest opportunity ahead.

MercadoPago is growing ~50% YoY with a long runway as financial services penetration across most of Latin America remains very low.

$NOW CEO Bill McDermott says AI can identify problems but it cannot fix them.

ServiceNow sits on 80B enterprise workflows across legacy systems and acts as the “last-mile” execution layer.

$UBER founder Travis Kalanick says wheeled robots will likely outperform humanoids like $TSLA Optimus for industrial tasks such as transport and mining.

He argues specialized robots designed for specific functions are far more efficient for large-scale operations.

Anduril wins a U.S. Army contract worth up to $20B.

The deal covers its AI-enabled Lattice platform along with integrated hardware, data infrastructure and technical support services for current and future defense systems.

$META is planning a new round of layoffs across the company.

Reports suggest the cuts could impact 20% or more of Meta’s workforce.
$MSFT is the first cloud provider to bring up an $NVDA Vera Rubin NVL72 system for validation
$META now trading under 13x 2028 operating profits despite expected ~20% revenue CAGR over the next three years with ~35% operating margins.

Regarding crude at $90 or $110 Monday. ...

Joint Chiefs Dan Caine said we are going to hit 6K targets today.  I assume the same on Saturday and Sunday.

So, Monday morning, I assume Caine says we hit 18K targets.

If we see no progress in getting control of the Strait... $110 Monday.

If this effort shows progress, not the Strait is "open," but "progress," ... $90.
If Iran does this, crude oil prices fall back to bear pre-war levels, and they lose the only leverage they have.
The only reason I do not allow my children to play in the middle of the highway is the cars speeding at 70 MPH. Otherwise, the highway is open as a playground.

War risk insurance was the problem last week, it has largely been resolved.

Technically the Strait is open and they have the insurance to transit it.

The problem is the Strait is being viewed as a "shooting gallery." Iran has hit 16 ships and killed more Mariners than he US Military has lost in this war,

No one wants to be the "duck."

Looking at Goldman Sachs Research gives a good timeline for thinking about the Strait of Hormuz

* March 4, Hormuz flows would recover in 5 days. 

* March 9, they pushed that to 10 days

* March 11, they extended it again to 21 days of very low flows before a 30-day gradual recovery.

Goldman's thinking was similar to mine. On March 4th, I ASSUMED the US Military had an active plan to open the Strait. 

Between March 4th and March 11th, my thinking (like Goldman's) evolved when we both realized they either had the wrong plan or no plan and are currently struggling to figure out what to do.

This is no longer a War with Iran
It is now a War to reopen the Strait of Hormuz

This is no longer a war against Iran.

It is now a war to open the Strait of Hormuz

He learned little/nothing from the drone warfare between Russia and Ukraine.

Or the Houthis in the Red Sea.

Or Hamas in Gaza against Israel.

They ignored the Ukrainians when they said they have a defensive option against drones.

Generals always prepare for the last war.

Meta says the MTIA 300 for R&R is already in deployment, while the 400 finished testing for genAI models.

That trade is stalling.

Q4 2025: small-cap value quietly outperformed growth for the first time in years.

Insurance. Regional banks. Business services. The boring stuff is waking up.

Energy stocks at all-time highs while $SPY sits at 2026 lows.

Exxon, Chevron, Marathon ripping. Everything else bleeding.

This isn't a market. It's a bifurcation hiding in plain sight.

Morgan Stanley's private credit fund: investors wanted 10.9% out. They got 5%.

Cliffwater's $33B fund: investors wanted 14% out. Got 7%.

Private credit is a roach motel. $MS

Gold above $5,100. Up 71% in a year.

Central banks aren't buying gold because they like the color.

They're buying it because they no longer trust the system they built. $GLD
I will not relent.

$VIX near 27.

Not panic. Not calm. Just enough fear to keep you from acting, not enough to force capitulation.

The worst kind of volatility is the kind that makes you freeze.

Brent above $100. WTI near $99.

Strait of Hormuz shut. 20% of global oil at risk. IEA released 400M barrels from reserves — the largest ever.

And it barely moved the needle.

Companies are staying private for longer...

The number of companies listed on U.S. exchanges has decreased dramatically since 1999...

Are you missing out on private market innovation because of this?

We believe so.

Today, multiple private companies like SpaceX and Anthropic rank among the top S&P 500 constituents.

How do you access this private market growth?

It's time to honor my father again.

An updated 3rd edition of his book Intermarket Analysis and Investing coming soon, covering the last 30 years with new indicators, historical analysis, and more. 

First published in 1990.

I republished it after he passed.

It's time.
